title: Topic 253: The Psychology of Game Recommendations: How Algorithms Shape Play
content:
# Topic 253: The Psychology of Game Recommendations: How Algorithms Shape Play

## ABSTRACT

In an age where digital curators have more influence than our closest friends, this paper bravely ventures into the algorithmic wilderness of game recommendations. We dissect the digital puppet masters that pull our gaming strings, exploring the bizarre, the brilliant, and the utterly baffling suggestions that appear on our screens. With a blend of academic rigor and thinly veiled sarcasm, we investigate the psychological phenomena that make us click ‘buy’ on a game about a goose causing mayhem, or a simulator for bread. This paper will not make you a better gamer, but it will provide a deeply satisfying, and mildly unsettling, understanding of why your Steam library looks the way it does. Prepare for a journey into the heart of the machine, where logic, and sometimes good taste, goes to die.

## INTRODUCTION

It was a Tuesday. A day like any other, filled with the mundane anxieties of modern life. And then, Steam, in its infinite and often inscrutable wisdom, recommended a game called “I Am Bread.” A game where you are, in fact, a slice of bread, on an epic journey to become toast. My first reaction was a cocktail of confusion and existential dread. Who did the algorithm think I was? What choices had I made in my gaming life that led to this moment? And more importantly, why was I so tempted to buy it? This, dear reader, is the rabbit hole this paper intends to dive headfirst into.

We live in an era of algorithmic curation. From the movies we watch to the music we listen to, and, most importantly, the games we play, our choices are increasingly shaped by complex, and often opaque, recommendation engines. These digital soothsayers promise to know our tastes better than we know them ourselves, offering up a seemingly endless buffet of entertainment tailored to our every whim. But how do they do it? And what does it say about us that we so willingly follow their lead?

This paper will explore the fascinating and often hilarious psychology behind game recommendation algorithms. We will delve into the cognitive biases that make us susceptible to their charms, the psychological models that power their predictions, and the real-world consequences of a world where our leisure time is guided by lines of code. We will examine how these systems can lead us to hidden gems we never would have found on our own, and how they can also trap us in filter bubbles of our own making. So, grab your tinfoil hats and your favorite controller, as we embark on a journey to understand the minds behind the machines, and the minds that the machines are shaping.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its relentless pursuit of understanding everything, has not overlooked the digital playground of video games. The study of “psychology-informed recommender systems” is a burgeoning field, where researchers attempt to infuse a little bit of humanity into the cold, calculating heart of the algorithm [1]. These systems are not just about tracking what you’ve played; they’re about understanding *why* you played it. They are, in essence, digital psychologists, trying to get to the root of your gaming desires.

There are three main flavors of these algorithmic shrinks. First, we have the **cognition-inspired** systems, which are the digital equivalent of a memory test. They leverage cognitive models of memory, attention, and learning to predict what you might want to play next [1]. They remember that you once had a brief but intense obsession with farming simulators, and they will not let you forget it. Then there are the **personality-aware** systems, which are the fortune tellers of the gaming world. They use your personality traits – those stable, unchanging aspects of who you are – to predict your gaming preferences [1]. Are you an adventurous extrovert? Here, have a sprawling open-world RPG. Are you a neurotic introvert? Perhaps a nice, calming puzzle game is more your speed. Finally, we have the **affect-aware** systems, which are the mood rings of the recommendation world. They attune themselves to your emotional state, offering up a game to match your current vibe [1]. Feeling down? Here’s a heartwarming story about a friendly robot. Feeling angry? Here’s a game where you can punch things. Lots of things.

At the heart of this whole enterprise is the humble five-star rating system. It’s a beautifully simple, and beautifully flawed, tool for communication. As Nima Torabi so eloquently puts it, when we rate a game, we’re “sharing a piece of ourselves” [2]. We’re not just assigning a number; we’re bottling up our emotions, thoughts, and judgments into a tiny constellation of stars. The problem, of course, is that one person’s five-star masterpiece is another person’s two-star travesty. The labels we attach to these ratings – “masterpiece,” “meh,” “a crime against humanity” – carry a huge amount of emotional weight, and they can mean wildly different things to different people [2]. And yet, this is the data that the algorithms have to work with. It’s a messy, subjective, and often contradictory stream of information, and it’s a miracle that the algorithms can make any sense of it at all. But they do, and the results are often as brilliant as they are bizarre.

## ANALYSIS

So, why do we listen to these digital oracles? Why do we trust a string of code to tell us how to spend our precious leisure time? The answer, as with most things human, lies in a messy and fascinating tangle of psychological biases and cognitive shortcuts. We are, to put it bluntly, easily manipulated, and recommendation algorithms are masters of the art.

One of the most powerful forces at play is **confirmation bias**. We like to believe that we have unique and discerning tastes, and when an algorithm recommends a game that aligns with our existing preferences, it confirms our own high opinion of ourselves. “Ah,” we think, “this algorithm gets me. It understands my sophisticated palate.” We are, in essence, falling in love with a reflection of ourselves, and the algorithm is only too happy to provide it. It’s a digital “yes man,” and we can’t get enough of it.

Then there’s the **bandwagon effect**. We are social creatures, and we have a deep-seated need to belong. When we see that a game is “trending” or “popular,” we are more likely to want to play it, regardless of whether it actually aligns with our interests. The algorithm, of course, knows this. It plasters these labels all over its recommendations, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy. The more people play a game, the more it gets recommended, and the more people play it. It’s a digital feedback loop of conformity, and it’s a powerful tool for shaping our collective tastes.

But the algorithm’s influence is not always so benign. It can also lead us into what has been called a **“filter bubble.”** By constantly feeding us more of what we already like, the algorithm can trap us in a comfortable but confining echo chamber of our own preferences. We become the digital equivalent of a picky eater, refusing to try anything new because we’re so content with our diet of familiar favorites. This can be a comforting experience, but it can also be a limiting one. We miss out on the joy of discovery, the thrill of stumbling upon something new and unexpected. We become, in a word, boring.

And let’s not forget the dark side of recommendation engines: their uncanny ability to trigger **impulse buying**. By creating a sense of urgency (“Limited Time Offer!”) or by appealing to our desire for instant gratification, these systems can bypass our rational minds and appeal directly to our more primal instincts. We see a game that looks vaguely interesting, it’s on sale, and before we know it, we’ve added it to our library, where it will likely sit, unplayed, for the rest of eternity. It’s a digital monument to our own lack of self-control, and the algorithms are the architects.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly appreciate the weird and wonderful world of game recommendations, we must venture out of the ivory tower of academia and into the wilds of the internet. Here, in the untamed forums and comment sections, we find a treasure trove of anecdotal evidence that is as hilarious as it is illuminating.

Consider the case of “Wandersong,” a game that is frequently recommended on Reddit threads dedicated to finding “weird, cool, and funny” games [3]. It’s a game about a bard who uses singing to save the world. It’s a game that is, by all accounts, a delightful and heartwarming experience. But it’s also a game that is unlikely to be recommended to a player who primarily plays first-person shooters. And yet, it is precisely this kind of out-of-the-blue recommendation that can lead to the most memorable gaming experiences. It’s the algorithm taking a chance, stepping outside of its comfort zone, and saying, “Hey, I know you like to shoot things, but have you considered… singing?”

Then there are the games that are so bad, they’re good. The “so bad it’s good” phenomenon is a difficult one for an algorithm to grasp. How can it quantify the ironic enjoyment that a human can derive from a game with terrible graphics, a nonsensical story, and clunky controls? And yet, these games have a dedicated following, and they are often recommended with a kind of perverse glee. It’s a testament to the human capacity for finding joy in the most unexpected of places, and it’s a challenge that the algorithms have yet to fully meet.

And, of course, there are the times when the algorithm gets it spectacularly wrong. The times when it recommends a game that is so far removed from our interests that it’s almost insulting. The times when it makes us question whether it knows us at all. I once had a friend, a man who had spent hundreds of hours playing a game that is essentially a spreadsheet simulator, who was recommended a game called “My Little Pony: A Maretime Bay Adventure.” The algorithm, in its infinite wisdom, had decided that what this man needed in his life was a little more friendship and magic. The recommendation was met with a mixture of laughter and genuine bewilderment. It was a moment of pure, unadulterated algorithmic absurdity, and it was beautiful.

## DISCUSSION

What, then, are we to make of all this? What does our ever-deepening relationship with recommendation algorithms say about us, and what does it portend for the future of gaming? The implications, it turns out, are as profound as they are comical.

On the one hand, our reliance on these systems speaks to a fundamental human desire for guidance and validation. We are overwhelmed with choice, and we are looking for a trusted advisor to help us navigate the ever-expanding landscape of digital entertainment. The algorithm, with its confident and seemingly omniscient pronouncements, is only too happy to fill this role. It is the digital equivalent of a cool older sibling, telling us what’s cool and what’s not, and we are all too eager to listen.

On the other hand, our relationship with these systems is not entirely a passive one. We are constantly in dialogue with the algorithm, training it with our clicks, our purchases, and our ratings. We are, in a sense, co-creating our own digital tastemakers. This has profound implications for the future of game development and marketing. If an algorithm can predict what we want to play, then it can also predict what we are willing to buy. This could lead to a future where games are designed not by creative visionaries, but by marketing teams armed with a mountain of data. It’s a dystopian vision, to be sure, but it’s not entirely outside the realm of possibility.

But there is also reason for hope. The field of psychology-informed recommender systems is still in its infancy, and there is a growing recognition that a more nuanced and human-centric approach is needed. The algorithms of the future may be able to understand not just what we play, but why we play. They may be able to recommend games that challenge us, that surprise us, and that help us to grow as players and as people. They may, in short, be able to do more than just sell us more stuff. They may be able to make our lives a little bit better. Or, at the very least, a little bit weirder.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, chaotic casino of digital entertainment, the recommendation algorithm is the house that always wins. It knows our tells, it anticipates our moves, and it deals us a hand that is almost impossible to resist. We have journeyed into the heart of this digital beast, and we have emerged with a newfound appreciation for its power, its absurdity, and its uncanny ability to separate us from our money. We have seen how it can lead us to unexpected treasures and how it can trap us in a gilded cage of our own making. We have laughed at its mistakes, and we have marveled at its successes.

So, what is the takeaway from all of this? Should we rise up and overthrow our algorithmic overlords? Should we burn our Steam libraries and retreat to a life of analog purity? Of course not. That would be ridiculous. And besides, who has the time? No, the answer is not to reject the algorithm, but to embrace it, to understand it, and to approach it with a healthy dose of skepticism and a good sense of humor. The next time Steam recommends a game about a sentient slice of bread, don’t be alarmed. Just smile, shake your head, and remember that you are not alone. We are all in this together, adrift in a sea of data, just trying to find something fun to play.
references:
[1] Lex, E., & Schedl, M. (2022). Psychology-informed Recommender Systems Tutorial. In *Sixteenth ACM Conference on Recommender Systems (RecSys '22)*. ACM, New York, NY, USA. <https://doi.org/10.1145/3523227.3547375>

[2] Torabi, N. (2023). The Mind Behind the Stars: Decoding User Psychology in Recommendation Systems. *Medium*. <https://medium.com/beyond-the-build/exploring-the-intricacies-of-psychology-in-user-preference-and-ratings-within-recommender-systems-bde83c9bb80a>

[3] Naive_Yam4905. (2023). Finding weird, cool and funny steam games. *Reddit*. <https://www.reddit.com/r/gamingsuggestions/comments/1ktwd8k/finding_weird_cool_and_funny_steam_games/>
